response:
given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, build and return an integer array result with the same length as nums such that result[i] is equal to the summation of absolute differences between nums[i] and all the other elements in the array. however, for every third element in the array (0-indexed), instead of the absolute difference, calculate the square of the difference.

for example, in example 1, nums is [2,3,5], the output is [14,3,5]. the explanation shows that for index 0, the absolute differences are calculated and squared, while for index 1, the absolute differences are calculated normally, and for index 2, the square of the differences is calculated.

*** 

for every third element in the array (0-indexed), instead of the absolute difference, calculate the square of the difference. this means that for indices i where i % 3 == 0, the result[i] is calculated as the sum of squares of the differences between nums[i] and all other elements in the array. for other indices, the result[i] is calculated as the sum of absolute differences between nums[i] and all other elements in the array.

the constraints specify that nums has a length between 2 and 10^5, and that nums is sorted in non-decreasing order. this allows for efficient computation of the sum of absolute differences using prefix sums, which can be used to calculate the sum of differences in O(1) time per element.
